A benign lie. We all do it. It doesn’t detach us from what’s true.

But bullshit is a completely different animal. A bullshitter isn’t trying to hide reality. They’re not even concerned with it. The only thing a BS statement serves is the moment itself.

If I want to borrow money from you, I’ll say I have none. Doesn’t matter what’s actually in my pocket. If I want to impress you, I’ll claim I have thousands. Same pocket, zero relationship with reality.

And here’s where it gets dark. Every BS statement you make is a dot. You make another one, then another, building a path that leads further and further from the road. At some point you’re so detached from reality that you completely lose your ability to recognize it. Your critical thinking doesn’t degrade. It evaporates.

I’m not exaggerating. Imagine me spending two years writing posts about how your notifications are engineered to keep you poor. First I’d say it for views. Then I’d say it because the engagement felt good.

Then I’d say it because I believed it. And then I’d be impossible to live with. Every article would look like propaganda. Every expert would feel like a shill. My brain would have lost the hardware for critical thinking entirely.

That’s brainrot. And it doesn’t start with conspiracy theories. It starts with casual, everyday indifference to truth.

Where BS Comes From

Two places, mainly.

First: public life. Any situation where you’re required to make statements about things you understand nothing about is a BS factory.

Think about it. Politicians who only understand voter behavior are suddenly making declarations about biology, neuroscience, space engineering, and climate science. They won an election, not a degree. But now they’re commenting on every domain of human knowledge with absolute confidence.

That’s not lying. That’s not truth. That’s just noise dressed up in a suit.

Second: the Dunning-Kruger effect. In the beginning, when you have very little knowledge about something, your confidence is at its peak. That’s where you’re most likely to make BS statements.

You don’t know much, someone asks you a question, you want to sound smart, so you fabricate. Not a lie. Not the truth. Just verbal filler that makes you feel competent for 30 seconds.

I’ve caught myself doing this. Two chais deep at a family dinner, an uncle asks how the economy actually works, and I start talking with way more confidence than my knowledge warrants. It feels good in the moment. Everyone nods. Then I go home and realize I basically made up half of what I said. Not maliciously. Just… carelessly.

Three Things I Now Do Differently

Frankfurt’s framework gave me a mental filter. Three rules I run things through now.

One. Before I make any statement, I ask myself: is this true, is this a lie, or is it neither? If it’s neither, I stop talking. Simple as that. Most BS dies at the moment you refuse to let it leave your mouth.

Two. I don’t have opinions about everything. One human cannot understand every single thing on this planet. If you ask me about the Israel-Palestine conflict, I’ll tell you I don’t understand it well enough to have a stance.

Neutral is a valid position. “I don’t know” is not a weakness. It’s a boundary that protects your brain from rotting.

Three. I read more. Books force you to sit with complexity. They don’t let you scroll past a contradiction. You have to hold multiple ideas in your head simultaneously, and that’s the exact exercise that builds critical thinking. Every book you read is a firmware update for your bullshit detector.

The Realization That Stuck

For years I thought the biggest threat to truth was dishonesty. People lying, cheating, hiding facts. Turns out that’s the wrong enemy entirely.

A liar still lives in reality. They just don’t want you to see it. A bullshitter has moved out and built their own. And if you spend enough time in a world where people speak without caring whether what they say is true, you stop being able to tell the difference yourself.

The most valuable thing I have isn’t anything I know. It’s the ability to look at a claim and ask: does this person care about what’s true? Because if they don’t, nothing they say matters. And if I stop caring, nothing I say will matter either.
entry.summary: I could write a blog post tomorrow claiming your phone notifications are quietly rewiring your brain to keep you broke. A confident tone, a few cherry-picked studies, some scary jargon. It would go viral. Guaranteed. And if I kept writing posts like that for a year or two, I’d eventually believe it myself.

entry.title: What a Rubik’s Cube and Car Wax Have to Do With My Keyboard Setup

link.title: What a Rubik’s Cube and Car Wax Have to Do With My Keyboard Setup

entry.content:
I have a Rubik’s cube in my desk drawer and a bottle of ceramic car wax under my sink. Neither of these things should have anything to do with keyboards.

And yet, that’s exactly what brought a 12-year-old TVS Gold back from the dead.

The Keychron K8 — modern, wireless, objectively the better keyboard on paper — has been a very expensive dust collector for three months. I don’t miss it.

I found the TVS Gold while shifting homes. It was buried in a box I hadn’t opened in years, the kind of box that’s secretly a graveyard of past-self decisions. I almost threw it out. But I pressed one of the keys and it made a sound that the Keychron, with all its modernity and its RGB lighting, has never made.

So it went in the keep pile.

Opening it up was the wrong decision and also the only possible decision. A decade of neglect had turned the inside into something I’d rather not describe in detail — dust, grime, and textures that shouldn’t exist. I cleaned it out with a brush and compressed air, and then I plugged it in to check if anything was worth saving.

Nothing happened.

I was ready to call it. Then I looked more carefully at the connector pins — green with corrosion. A bit of IPA, some rewiring that I’d charitably call creative, a prayer to no god in particular, and I plugged it in again.

Every key worked. The thing was just waiting.

The guts being alive meant facing the exterior, which was a problem. The plastic frame had gone from black to a mottled grey-brown — not from dust, but from the plastic itself degrading.

I dismantled the whole thing. The board cleaned up easily enough.

The keycaps did not. I scrubbed every single one by hand. All 104 of them. I was crouched over my sink for longer than I want to admit, and somewhere around keycap 70 I had a very clear thought: I could just buy a new keyboard. I kept going anyway.

Clean keys, functional board — but the switches felt wrong. Stiff, scratchy, the kind of tactile experience that makes you not want to type. They needed lubing.

On a new board, I’d desolder each switch and do it properly. This was a 12-year-old board I’d just barely coaxed back to life, and my soldering is, generously, a work in progress. I was not about to risk it.

So I did something I’m not entirely proud of: I grabbed a syringe and reached for the silicone lube I use on my Rubik’s cubes.

This is, apparently, a sin in keyboard circles. I found this out after the fact, which was good, because by then the keys were buttery smooth and I didn’t care. The lube that keeps a cube spinning through fingertips at speed turns out to be exactly what these switches wanted.

The keys were fixed. The frame was still an eyesore — clean, but faded and sad, the plastic dull in the way old plastic gets when it’s given up on being black.

I’d tried the conventional suggestions and wasn’t excited about any of them. Then I thought about the ceramic wax I use on my car. It’s designed to fill microscopic scratches and bond to clear coat, but the base mechanism is just restoring surface integrity.

Worth trying.

The result was genuinely surprising. Not just shinier — the actual black came back. Deep, even, close to what it must have looked like new.

So that’s what a Rubik’s cube and a car have to do with keyboards.

The TVS Gold has been my daily driver for three months. It gets more comments than anything else on my desk. And the original blue switches — the ones that made that sound when I found it in the box — they’re still doing exactly that.

TVS used to build switches like this. Their current lineup doesn’t come close. I don’t know if that’s a manufacturing decision or a cost decision or just time passing, but I’m glad this one survived long enough for me to find it in a box I almost threw away.
entry.summary: I have a Rubik’s cube in my desk drawer and a bottle of ceramic car wax under my sink. Neither of these things should have anything to do with keyboards.
